title: Pen care and maintenance
subtitle: Cleaning, storing, and reviving dip nibs and pen holders
excerpt: A dip nib is a simple piece of pressed steel, but it corrodes quickly if ink is left to dry in it, and it performs inconsistently if the nib slit is clogged. Ten minutes of correct maintenance after each session extends nib life, keeps the ink flow clean, and prevents the most common problems beginners blame on their technique.
sourceNotes: Standard calligraphy workshop practice, synthesised from Margaret Shepherd, Learn Calligraphy (2001, Broadway Books), and Gourdie, Italic Handwriting (1955), tool-care appendices.

glossaryTerms.definition: The narrow cut running from the nib's tip to its vent hole. It acts as a capillary channel, drawing ink down to the tip by surface tension.
A small metal tab clipped onto a broad-edge nib that holds extra ink and feeds it gradually to the slit, allowing longer strokes between dips.

content.text: is small enough that a single session's worth of dried ink can seal it completely. Most beginners who think they have a bad nib actually have an incompletely cleaned nib from the previous session.

Remove the nib from the holder. Hold it in water for 30 seconds to loosen dried ink. Wipe gently on a damp cloth, wipe from the back of the nib toward the tip, never tip-to-back, which pushes ink into the slit. Check the slit with a fingernail: it should move very slightly under light pressure. If it doesn't, soak for another 60 seconds. If using iron-gall ink, wash with a small amount of dish soap; iron-gall leaves a darker stain than carbon inks but is not harmful to the steel if washed promptly.

Dry the nib by blotting on a cloth, don't blow on it, as moisture from breath can introduce salts that accelerate rust. Store nibs in a small tin or box out of direct sunlight, away from humidity. A few grains of dry silica gel in the storage tin keeps moisture out. Do not store nibs in a jar together with pen holders: the jostling dulls the tip.

clip on a broad-edge nib should touch the top face of the nib about 2 mm back from the tip. If the reservoir is pushed too far forward it blocks the slit; too far back and it doesn't feed. Remove the reservoir to clean it separately, ink stacks in the reservoir slot and dries thickly. Use a folded corner of cartridge to poke the slot clean, then rinse.

New nibs are coated with a fine protective oil to prevent rust during storage. Ink sits on top of this oil rather than flowing into the slit. Remove the coating before first use: pass the nib briefly through a candle flame for two seconds (until any oil burns off), or clean it with a cotton bud dipped in isopropyl alcohol. After treatment, dip the nib in ink and check the flow immediately, it should be immediate and even.

A nib that has been stored dirty for months can usually be revived with a 20-minute soak in warm water with a drop of dish soap, then a gentle scrub with an old soft toothbrush. If the tip is corroded (visible as a roughness that catches on paper), the nib is finished, replace it. Good nibs from reputable makers at modest price mean there is no economic reason to persist with a corroded one.
